# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate` -->

Generated: 2026-05-26T16:39:38Z

## Testing Standards

- pytest is the test framework. Tests live in tests/ and include fixture-based parser unit tests, an age round-trip suite, a launchd plist plutil-lint test, and a real-export regression for Health Connect. Any code that touches health data ships with at least one fixture-based test, and `pytest -q` must be green before review handoff. The `@pytest.mark.regression` suite may be skipped when real export files are absent. Test-first development is required for new behavior (DIRECTIVE_034): a failing test must precede the production code that motivates it; cycles run in minutes, not hours; horizontal slicing -- writing a batch of tests before any implementation -- is prohibited because it codifies imagined behavior. Tests verify behavior through public interfaces (DIRECTIVE_036): inside-boundary collaborators are not patched or imported to drive business logic; assertions target observable outputs (CLI exits, DuckDB row counts, file bytes, raised exceptions). Outside-boundary substitutes -- subprocess wrappers around `age`/`rclone`, `shutil.which`, config parameters -- remain permitted.


## Quality Gates

- Before review handoff: ruff (lint + format) clean, mypy clean for the changed scope, `pytest -q` green. Code must stay modular -- avoid monolithic or excessively interconnected designs so that small changes do not ripple into unrelated behavior. Pre-existing failures unrelated to the change must be called out explicitly, not hidden. Static type hints are expected on public functions; runtime input validation lives at the parser, CLI, and agent boundary.


## Performance Benchmarks

- User-experience priorities are first-class: minimize cognitive load, ground every health-related claim in real science, communicate with empathy, and never overstate diagnostic confidence -- the app suggests questions to bring to a doctor; it does not diagnose. Soft technical targets (from SPEC §4 NFR-Performance): non-ingest CLI verbs respond in under 2 s; full Health Connect ingest (~200 MB export) under 60 s on M-series Mac; monthly end-to-end run under 10 minutes for a ~500 MB zip; warehouse stays under 1 GB after five years of history.


## Branch Strategy

- At least one focused reviewer approves before merging. The maintainer self-reviews via the spec-kitty implement-review loop (kitty-specs/* work packages). External contributions arrive as GitHub PRs and require maintainer approval plus all quality gates green before merge. CI is not configured today; review correctness is the merge gate. Conventional commits are not required; commit messages should be descriptive and reference the spec-kitty work package or doctrine action when applicable.

- Deployment constraints: Primary platform: macOS (launchd-scheduled monthly run). Linux and Windows are out of the pre-`v1` scope, but the warehouse file and encrypted artifacts MUST remain platform-portable (stock age + DuckDB >=1.1). master is the single long-lived branch; feature work happens in spec-kitty worktrees under .worktrees/ and is merged back to master via the spec-kitty merge workflow. Release artifacts are git tags on the `v0.x.0` line until Premura has a coherent user-facing path across all four stages; the existing `v1.0.0` tag is historical and MUST NOT set the forward version line. Every phase or release mission MUST end with a version/doc/tag gate: update the package/docs release line, record validation evidence, and cut the planned git tag unless the maintainer explicitly defers it. Semver applies once external users exist. No CI/CD, no container, no server-side deploy today -- this is a local-first Python toolchain installed via uv. The MCP/tool surface is the default analytical interface, while CLI, DuckDB, and notebook use remain operator or expert fallback paths. Any future agent-facing layer inherits the same local-first stance unless explicitly amended.


## Governance Activation

```yaml
mission: software-dev
selected_paradigms: []
selected_directives: [DIRECTIVE_010, DIRECTIVE_024, DIRECTIVE_028, DIRECTIVE_030, DIRECTIVE_033, DIRECTIVE_034, DIRECTIVE_036]
available_tools: [spec-kitty, git]
template_set: software-dev-default
```

## Policy Summary

- Intent: Premura is a local-first, agent-operable health reasoning substrate. The agent is the primary operational client; the human is the primary beneficiary. A human supplies source artifacts, questions, goals, and approvals; the agent does most of the internal work: ingesting, normalizing, selecting signals, analyzing, comparing, explaining, and proposing extensions. The purpose is to help a person understand what might be going on in their own health data so they can bring grounded, evidence-backed questions to a doctor and ask for the right follow-up tests or help. This still serves people who are afraid to go to the doctor in case something is wrong and people who have already seen many doctors without receiving a proper diagnosis or feeling listened to. The system's interpretations must be grounded in real science and must never overstate diagnostic confidence. The current technical foundation is a local-first Python pipeline (DuckDB warehouse, age-encrypted artifacts) that ingests monthly GDPR-style health exports; the shipped MCP/tool surface is already the default analytical path, while direct SQL or notebook work remains an expert fallback.

- Design Altitude (core principle): Premura is operated and extended by AI agents, so specs, contracts, and docs MUST be written a level above the concrete case — as bounded abstractions agents fill in (registries, rubrics, contracts, allowlists-with-rules), not as exhaustive enumerations of domains, metrics, questions, or policies. At specify/plan time and again at review, apply the test: does this artifact hardcode a list where it should define the rule for adding to the list? Avoid both failure modes: too broad (agents invent their own conventions and PRs become unreviewable) and too narrow (agents find the design over-constraining and violate or route around it). The target is the middle — guide agents to create their own policies without strictly limiting them. The federated parser seam (PluginParser contract + suggest_metric resolution order + unmapped_metrics surface) is the reference pattern; an enumerated `if source == ...` ladder is the anti-pattern. Full statement with examples: docs/product/DOCTRINE.md.

- Languages/Frameworks: Python 3.11+ (single language; no frontend yet). Core libraries: typer + rich (CLI), pydantic + pydantic-settings (config), DuckDB >=1.1, polars (parse + transform), structlog (logging), jinja2 (launchd plist), pyarrow, python-ulid. Dev tooling: uv (package + virtualenv manager), pytest + pytest-cov, ruff (lint + format), mypy (types). New surfaces such as agent-facing layers must justify any additional language or framework.

- Testing: pytest is the test framework. Tests live in tests/ and include fixture-based parser unit tests, an age round-trip suite, a launchd plist plutil-lint test, and a real-export regression for Health Connect. Any code that touches health data ships with at least one fixture-based test, and `pytest -q` must be green before review handoff. The `@pytest.mark.regression` suite may be skipped when real export files are absent. Test-first development is required for new behavior (DIRECTIVE_034): a failing test must precede the production code that motivates it; cycles run in minutes, not hours; horizontal slicing -- writing a batch of tests before any implementation -- is prohibited because it codifies imagined behavior. Tests verify behavior through public interfaces (DIRECTIVE_036): inside-boundary collaborators are not patched or imported to drive business logic; assertions target observable outputs (CLI exits, DuckDB row counts, file bytes, raised exceptions). Outside-boundary substitutes -- subprocess wrappers around `age`/`rclone`, `shutil.which`, config parameters -- remain permitted.

- Quality Gates: Before review handoff: ruff (lint + format) clean, mypy clean for the changed scope, `pytest -q` green. Code must stay modular -- avoid monolithic or excessively interconnected designs so that small changes do not ripple into unrelated behavior. Pre-existing failures unrelated to the change must be called out explicitly, not hidden. Static type hints are expected on public functions; runtime input validation lives at the parser, CLI, and agent boundary.

- Review Policy: At least one focused reviewer approves before merging. The maintainer self-reviews via the spec-kitty implement-review loop (kitty-specs/* work packages). External contributions arrive as GitHub PRs and require maintainer approval plus all quality gates green before merge. CI is not configured today; review correctness is the merge gate. Conventional commits are not required; commit messages should be descriptive and reference the spec-kitty work package or doctrine action when applicable.

- Performance Targets: User-experience priorities are first-class: minimize cognitive load, ground every health-related claim in real science, communicate with empathy, and never overstate diagnostic confidence -- the app suggests questions to bring to a doctor; it does not diagnose. Soft technical targets (from SPEC §4 NFR-Performance): non-ingest CLI verbs respond in under 2 s; full Health Connect ingest (~200 MB export) under 60 s on M-series Mac; monthly end-to-end run under 10 minutes for a ~500 MB zip; warehouse stays under 1 GB after five years of history.

- Deployment Constraints: Primary platform: macOS (launchd-scheduled monthly run). Linux and Windows are out of the pre-`v1` scope, but the warehouse file and encrypted artifacts MUST remain platform-portable (stock age + DuckDB >=1.1). master is the single long-lived branch; feature work happens in spec-kitty worktrees under .worktrees/ and is merged back to master via the spec-kitty merge workflow. Release artifacts are git tags on the `v0.x.0` line until Premura has a coherent user-facing path across all four stages; the existing `v1.0.0` tag is historical and MUST NOT set the forward version line. Every phase or release mission MUST end with a version/doc/tag gate: update the package/docs release line, record validation evidence, and cut the planned git tag unless the maintainer explicitly defers it. Semver applies once external users exist. No CI/CD, no container, no server-side deploy today -- this is a local-first Python toolchain installed via uv. The MCP/tool surface is the default analytical interface, while CLI, DuckDB, and notebook use remain operator or expert fallback paths. Any future agent-facing layer inherits the same local-first stance unless explicitly amended.


## Project Directives

1. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_010` to planning and implementation decisions.
2. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_024` to planning and implementation decisions.
3. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_028` to planning and implementation decisions.
4. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_030` to planning and implementation decisions.
5. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_033` to planning and implementation decisions.
6. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_034` to planning and implementation decisions.
7. Apply doctrine directive `DIRECTIVE_036` to planning and implementation decisions.
8. Respect risk boundaries: Non-negotiable governance boundaries for this project: (1) No live API scraping for personal health data -- ingestion uses official user-initiated exports only (GDPR dumps, HC auto-export files, app-export CSVs). python-garminconnect-style logins, Google Fit REST, and Apple HealthKit are out of scope. (2) Local-first and offline by default -- the pipeline MUST run end-to-end with no network. Cloud upload is opt-in (hpipe upload), never part of the automated run. hpipe doctor and all read paths MUST NOT phone home. Any agent-facing layer added later inherits this stance. (3) Scientific grounding -- any health interpretation surfaced to the user (including via agents) MUST cite evidence and MUST NOT overstate diagnostic confidence. The system suggests questions and next steps; it does not diagnose. (4) Human-on-the-loop approvals -- agents may do most internal analytical and integration work autonomously after the human supplies artifacts and goals, but any sensitive external action, policy change, charter or governance change, or over-the-boundary health claim requires explicit human approval first. (5) PHI hygiene -- no PHI in logs, tests, or commits; age-encryption is mandatory before any cloud upload; age.key permissions are chmod 0600.

9. Design a level above — guide, don't enumerate. Specs, contracts, and docs MUST express bounded abstractions agents fill in (registries, rubrics, contracts), not exhaustive lists of domains, metrics, questions, or policies. Self-check at specify/plan/review: does this hardcode a list where it should define the rule for adding to the list? See the Design Altitude policy above and docs/product/DOCTRINE.md.

10. Keep documentation synchronized with workflow and behavior changes.

| `DIRECTIVE:DIRECTIVE_033` | directive | Agent-authored commits must stage only the expected deliverables for the current work package. Blanket staging commands that could inadvertently include unrelated changes, secrets, or build artefacts are prohibited.
 | `_LIBRARY/directive-directive-033.md` |
| `DIRECTIVE:DIRECTIVE_034` | directive | Require test-first behavior across acceptance and implementation layers, selecting tactics based on scope and risk. Tests define the contract before production code is written.
 | `_LIBRARY/directive-directive-034.md` |
| `DIRECTIVE:DIRECTIVE_036` | directive | Exercise the system under test through its external interfaces only, without reference to its internal code structure, implementation details, or internal paths. Test cases derive from specifications, requirements, and design parameters, and assertions are made on observable outputs returned through those external interfaces. "Black-box" refers to the technique (absence of structural knowledge) and is independent of test scope — the scope is selected separately by responsibility, not by default to end-to-end.
 | `_LIBRARY/directive-directive-036.md` |
| `TACTIC:acceptance-test-first` | tactic | Anchor implementation work in externally observable behavior before internal coding begins. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-acceptance-test-first.md` |
| `TACTIC:black-box-integration-testing` | tactic | Validate the functionality of an application under test without peering into its internal structures or workings, by driving it through an external interface and asserting on observable outputs. Tests are derived from external descriptions of the software — specifications, requirements, and design parameters — rather than from its code. "Black-box" describes the testing technique (no structural knowledge of the system under test), not the size of the slice under test. Applied here at the integration boundary — exercising more than one collaborator together through a single external interface — but the same discipline also applies at the module, acceptance, and system levels.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-black-box-integration-testing.md` |
| `TACTIC:change-apply-smallest-viable-diff` | tactic | Introduce changes using the smallest possible modification that achieves the stated goal, minimising unintended side effects. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-change-apply-smallest-viable-diff.md` |
| `TACTIC:code-review-incremental` | tactic | Review a change set to identify correctness, structural, and architectural risks without rewriting or expanding scope. Formulate feedback as observations and questions, not instructions. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-code-review-incremental.md` |
| `TACTIC:quality-gate-verification` | tactic | Verify the relevant test and static validation gates for a change before review handoff and distinguish introduced failures from pre-existing debt. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-quality-gate-verification.md` |
| `TACTIC:review-intent-and-risk-first` | tactic | Review work by first understanding intent and surfacing risks before suggesting changes or solutions. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-review-intent-and-risk-first.md` |
| `TACTIC:tdd-red-green-refactor` | tactic | Enforce short, verifiable coding loops that improve design while keeping behavior safe. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-tdd-red-green-refactor.md` |
| `TACTIC:test-boundaries-by-responsibility` | tactic | Determine test scope by identifying which components are directly responsible for the behavior under test, rather than following structural layer boundaries. Reduces brittle tests while ensuring meaningful validation. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-test-boundaries-by-responsibility.md` |
| `TACTIC:test-pyramid-progression` | tactic | Run tests bottom-up through pyramid layers to get fast feedback and avoid wasting time on slow tests when fast tests fail. The execution order mirrors the ROI order: the cheapest, fastest tests run first because they catch the most bugs per second of CI time.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-test-pyramid-progression.md` |
| `TACTIC:testing-select-appropriate-level` | tactic | Choose the right test level (unit, integration, system) for a change based on scope of impact, risk of failure, and feedback cost. Avoids both over-testing low-risk changes and under-testing high-risk ones. Grounded in the test pyramid model where ROI decreases as test scope widens.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-testing-select-appropriate-level.md` |
| `TACTIC:zombies-tdd` | tactic | Drive implementation through tiny behavior increments using failing tests to control complexity. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-zombies-tdd.md` |
| `TOOLGUIDE:efficient-local-tooling` | toolguide | Operation-oriented guidance for using efficient local tools during repository work. | `_LIBRARY/toolguide-efficient-local-tooling.md` |

## Amendment Process

The charter is amended by editing .kittify/charter/charter.md directly and running `spec-kitty charter sync --json`. Material amendments (changing a risk boundary, lowering a quality gate, adding or removing a directive) MUST be committed in a dedicated commit whose message starts with "charter:" and links to the spec-kitty mission or issue that motivated the change. Editorial fixes (typos, clarifications) may ride in any commit. The interview answers in this file are the editable source for regeneration; if the charter diverges from these answers, regenerate rather than hand-edit downstream YAML.


## Exception Policy

Quality-gate exceptions (e.g., a known-failing mypy line that is pre-existing scope) MUST be called out explicitly in the work package or PR description with a one-line rationale; silent skips are not permitted. Risk-boundary exceptions (live API access, online-only behavior, unsourced or overconfident health claims) are not allowed under this charter -- a feature that requires breaching one of the risk boundaries requires a charter amendment first. PHI-handling security requirements (encryption, key permissions, no-PHI-in-logs) admit no exceptions.
